video_title: Policy Intervention Is Keeping The Bull Market Alive | Weekly Roundup
gist: The hosts argue the bull market is being sustained by deliberate policy intervention (a Bessent/Trump playbook of jawboning Iran/FX to crush volatility into the SpaceX IPO), that the ~2 rate hikes priced into the curve are wrong given negative real wages and oil-driven inflation, and that the most asymmetric trade is long SOFR futures plus long gold. They are bearish the Mag 7 hyperscalers as equity issuance turns net-positive and bullish the AI supplier complex (memory, power, Bitcoin-miner sites) the hyperscalers fund.

insights.claim: The Trump administration runs a repeatable 'statecraft' playbook: escalate geopolitical fear (Iran) to spike vol and weaken FX, then de-escalate with a deal to crush volatility and rally markets, a pattern the hosts say has repeated roughly four times.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: Treat geopolitical fear spikes as fade-able; buy risk into engineered panic and trim into the announced deal.
insights.quote: we're watching centralized asset management play out where you can control the market like that off positioning

insights.claim: The Iran de-escalation was timed so as not to tank markets into the SpaceX IPO, with the announcement deliberately landing to crush vol before a likely sell-the-news event.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: Watch IPO calendars as a tell for the timing of policy interventions and vol-crush rallies.
insights.quote: He wasn't going to set the world on fire into the SpaceX IPO.

insights.claim: Scott Bessent is credited as the architect of FX intervention, repeatedly de-escalating geopolitical threats when USD/JPY approaches 160 to relieve pressure on equities.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: USD/JPY near 160 is a trip-wire for policy intervention; position for mean-reversion in the carry trade around that level.
insights.quote: it's Bessen I mean obviously like it's the hedge fund manager running the treasury because yen USD JPY gets up to 160

insights.claim: Next week's BOJ and FOMC meetings are the key risk events; the speaker's gut is USD/JPY must break higher above 160, and authorities likely cannot keep a lid on it without causing equity weakness.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: A USD/JPY break above 160 is the catalyst to watch for a carry-trade unwind and equity drawdown.
insights.quote: my gut says it's got to go but I would say that they want to try and bat it down one more time

insights.claim: Markets no longer sell off on Trump's geopolitical threats because participants assume he will reverse, making threats lose market impact and forcing the need for new tactics.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Diminishing returns on the playbook mean the next intervention may need to be larger or different; watch for a regime change in the tactic.
insights.quote: even prior to this like the market didn't really go down on his big threats because no one believed it anyway

themes.theme: Rates, inflation and the SOFR/gold trade

insights.claim: The market has swung from pricing multiple cuts pre-war to roughly two hikes by mid-2027, which the hosts call max asymmetric hawkishness and a high-conviction fade.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: Long SOFR futures is asymmetric: you win unless two hikes materialize by mid next year, an unlikely outcome.
insights.quote: We've hit max asymmetric hawkishness. I don't know how we get any more than what we're at right now.

insights.claim: A sustained inflationary cycle is unlikely without wage growth, and real wages are currently negative, undercutting the case for the hikes priced into the curve.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Fade rate-hike pricing: the wage-price-spiral driver that justified the 2021-22 hikes is absent now.
insights.quote: I just don't know how you get this pronounced inflationary cycle without wage growth.

insights.claim: Current inflation is mostly commodity, energy and supply-shock inflation that self-corrects via market equilibrium rather than requiring a Fed reaction, especially if Hormuz reopens and oil drops.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: If Hormuz opens, headline inflation falls fast, exposing the Fed as behind on easing; bullish duration and SOFR.

insights.claim: Two-year breakevens were falling while nominal yields stayed flat, meaning real yields rose and liquidity was drained, implying the Fed is actually too tight and behind on easing.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Rising real yields with falling breakevens signal policy is too tight; positions for an eventual dovish surprise.
insights.quote: real real yields were actually growing meaning liquidity was kind of getting taken out of the market

insights.claim: Kevin Warsh is widely expected to debut at next week's FOMC as a turbo hawk and gold is selling off on that consensus, but his hearing rhetoric (trimmed-mean and core measures) suggests he is less hawkish than priced.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: If Warsh is less hawkish than feared, gold's selloff reverses, a contrarian long-gold setup.
insights.quote: the market has fully bonded to this idea of Kevin Worf coming in next week to his first meeting and just being a turbo hawk

insights.claim: Even a flat Fed amounts to a passive ease because, absent hikes, the SOFR market must eventually price out the embedded hikes to return to neutral, making the asymmetry one-sided toward lower rates.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: The SOFR long wins on a hold, a cut, or a growth scare; only realized hikes lose.

insights.claim: A long gold position is a reflection of the same SOFR thesis and is expected to perform well as hike pricing unwinds.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: Pair gold long with SOFR long as expressions of the rate-hikes-won't-happen view.
insights.quote: I think a gold long is the same reflection of that trade and I think it can do really well

insights.claim: Growth and inflation are likely making a local top here, with risk of an earnings hangover when Q2 results arrive mid-July through August.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Position defensively into July-August earnings; the real economy can't handle real rates at current levels.
insights.quote: To me, it's likely a local top in both growth and inflation right here.

themes.theme: Derivatives structure and the dispersion unwind

insights.claim: One-month implied correlation collapsed to about 6 while VIX sat sub-16 and single-stock vol skyrocketed, an extreme dispersion trade that violently unwound when oil/Iran triggered a VIX spike, wiping out retail call buyers.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: Extreme low implied correlation flags a fragile dispersion trade primed to unwind; fade meme/single-stock euphoria at those readings.
insights.quote: it was at six implied correlation one month implied correlation

insights.claim: Per Goldman's Cullen, S&P one-week downside vol rose 12 points in five sessions and the VIX term structure inverted as everyone bought short-dated downside protection, historically a contrarian buy signal because the market is over-hedged.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: An inverted VIX curve equals over-protection equals buy risk; a super-squeeze is likely as delevered CTAs re-lever.
insights.quote: S&P one side oneweek downside volumes up 12 points in just five sessions

insights.claim: Because CTAs delevered into the vol spike and a lot of protection was bought, a de-escalation plus low oil and a Fed behind on easing could trigger a super squeeze higher.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: Position for a sharp squeeze rally as systematic funds re-lever after the vol crush.
insights.quote: You could get a super squeeze cuz I think a lot of these CTAs delevered.

insights.claim: The repeating violent rallies are a structural consequence of a centralized, beta-neutral asset-management industry of large pension, quant and systematic funds incentivized not to lose, and it won't change until capital decentralizes.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: Use the structure: buy cheap insurance when vol is low and buy risk when the VIX curve inverts.
insights.quote: It's the direct result of centralizing the asset management industry.

insights.claim: Leveraged ETFs have grown large enough that algorithms front-run their end-of-day rebalancing flow, making early-afternoon policy announcements extra squeezy into the close in both directions.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: Time entries around afternoon catalysts; the close amplifies the day's move via mechanical rebalancing.
insights.quote: the algorithms in are just front running that flow

themes.theme: Mag 7 versus the AI supplier complex

insights.claim: The MAG7 ETF is down year-to-date while the rest of the NASDAQ (ex-Mag7) is up about 12% YTD, as record equity issuance and the reversal of buybacks weigh on the megacaps.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Underweight the megacaps relative to equal-weight; the buyback-to-issuance flip is an underappreciated drag.
insights.quote: the MAG7 ETF is down year to date versus like NASDAQ's XMAG7 is up 12% year to date

insights.claim: Hyperscalers are flooding the market with equity issuance (~$75B SpaceX, ~$40B Oracle, $80B Google, plus CoreWeave's $3.5B), turning a decade of net-negative equity supply positive for the first time and pressuring prices.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: Rising equity supply mechanically caps megacap returns like a crypto token unlock; favor the suppliers receiving that capital.
insights.quote: dev is hitting the mint button now on supply

insights.claim: The trade is to avoid the hyperscalers and buy what they buy with their debt and equity proceeds; memory is up about 3x over recent months while Mag7 is down YTD.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Own the AI-supplier and memory complex funded by hyperscaler capex, not the megacaps issuing the paper.
insights.quote: buy the things that the hyperscalers are buying funded by the things that they're selling which is their debt and their equity

insights.claim: A $6 trillion company cannot justify a growth multiple because matching the implied growth would require it to exceed US GDP within a few years, making 'Nvidia is cheap on multiples' claims fundamentally unsound.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Size is the ceiling on megacap multiples; be skeptical of cheap-multiple bull cases on the largest names.
insights.quote: a $6 trillion company should not trade at a growth multiple

insights.claim: The next leg is that the only way Mag7 hyperscalers stop their share underperformance is to curtail low-ROI capex, and when they do, cracks across the broader NASDAQ complex will appear.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: A capex slowdown would relieve megacaps but hurt the supplier complex; watch capex guidance as the regime-change signal.
insights.quote: The only way you stop the mag seven hypers scale or share price underperformance is by curtailing the thing the market is punishing you for which is your drunken spending.

insights.claim: High-yield credit spreads have been essentially unchanged for months despite the equity vol; the recent selloff was an equity-market phenomenon, not a credit event, with AI-builder bonds trading near par.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: Credit is not confirming equity stress; a credit cycle would only end if the Iran/oil shock had been pushed further.
insights.quote: if you looked at the high yield bond market over the last, you know, week or two, it really didn't move that much

themes.theme: AI buildout, power and Bitcoin mining

insights.claim: AI power economics are so strong that data-center developers are knocking on Bitcoin miners' doors for their already-secured power, while many non-miner firms claiming gigawatts of capacity don't actually have grid access.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: Bitcoin miners with real power are AI-infrastructure call options; verify claimed gigawatts against grid reality.
insights.quote: These guys are knocking down my door for AI because all the Bitcoin miners, they have the power.

insights.claim: Bitcoin's price weakness is partly driven by miners selling BTC and converting hash power to AI compute, with network hashrate falling over the past year as rigs pivot to AI.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: A supply overhang from miner BTC sales pressures Bitcoin until power economics equilibrate between mining and AI.
insights.quote: the the miners are actually transitioning to to their power to AI and it's winning the battle now

insights.claim: China is committing roughly $300B USD to AI infrastructure and is ahead of the US on the power side, making the buildout a national-policy race the US is incentivized to keep funding.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: The AI-war framing supports continued policy backing of US capex despite political pushback.

insights.claim: Political and physical bottlenecks to the buildout are emerging: data-center moratoriums (e.g., Utah), a halted Crusoe project in Wyoming, and rising anti-data-center and anti-electricity-bill sentiment heading into midterms about five months out.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: Local opposition and grid limits are a real tail risk to the capex narrative; track moratorium news flow.

insights.claim: AI token and inference costs are rising to the point of exceeding the cost of individual employees in some cases (Uber reportedly overspending and resetting), suggesting adoption is not a frictionless straight line.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: Rising inference economics could pressure AI-adoption ROI and corporate spend assumptions.

insights.claim: Speculation that Trump may take government stakes in AI companies to lower their cost of capital for the buildout, echoing a Bernie Sanders proposal, would be a potential catalyst but raises monopoly and centralization concerns.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: Government equity stakes would be a bullish catalyst for chosen AI names but a governance red flag for investors.
insights.quote: imagine Trump starts taking stakes in certain companies to like lower their cost of capital

themes.theme: AI model access, regulatory capture and macro politics

insights.claim: Anthropic's new 'Fable' model is described as a safeguarded, restricted version of its flagship 'Mythos', creating a two-tier system where only an approved short-list gets unrestrained access while others can't do meaningful work, raising regulatory-capture and innovation-stifling concerns.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: Model-access gatekeeping is a political and regulatory flashpoint that could shape AI policy and competitive dynamics into midterms.
insights.quote: if you're part of the the short list that Anthropic improves, yes, you get unrestrained mythos access but then for the rest of the permanent underclass you can't really do anything meaningful with it

insights.claim: David Sacks and the Trump admin are credited with raising alarms about Anthropic's regulatory-capture push, framing AI access policy as a contested issue inside the administration.
insights.speaker: host (Felix)
insights.so_what: Intra-administration friction on AI gatekeeping signals policy is unsettled, a source of headline risk for AI names.
insights.quote: David Saxs has been ringing the alarm about anthropic playing regulatory capture

insights.claim: The administration's actions (Iran war, ~80% gas-price spike, failed and reversed tariffs with no remaining tariff revenue) run opposite to what would maximize midterm success, fueling speculation about why markets remain the top priority five months out.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: The disconnect between policy and electoral incentives is a key uncertainty; populist pivots before midterms remain a wildcard.
insights.quote: none of their actions align with what someone would do to increase their odds of winning the midterms

insights.claim: If Leopold Aschenbrenner's thesis holds (20-30% GDP growth from AI), the resulting productivity could fund a vastly larger social safety net, the bull case for the buildout despite middle-class displacement.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: The optimistic AI-growth scenario underpins the case for staying long the AI economy through volatility.

insights.claim: The overarching investing philosophy is to use the government's incentives against it: front-run policy-driven dilution and inflation, step aside when markets get frothy, and buy back when blood is in the streets and nominal growth must reaccelerate.
insights.speaker: host (quant)
insights.so_what: A repeatable tactical playbook for a policy-driven, centrally managed market: fade extremes in both directions.
insights.quote: try to frontr run everything they're going to do so you can stay ahead of the the monetary dilution and inflation
